title: Types of Data
concept: Data comes in different types — numbers, categories, text, images, audio. The type decides what you can do with it.

exercises.prompt: Recap: data is best described as…?
exercises.explain: Right — data is recorded information of any kind. Now let's see the different kinds.

cards.title: Not all data is the same
cards.body: A temperature, a name, a selfie, a voice note — all data, but completely different kinds. The kind changes what you can do with it.

cards.title: Numbers (quantitative)
cards.body: Things you can measure or count: age, price, steps, temperature. You can add and average them.

cards.title: Categories (qualitative)
cards.body: Labels and groups: color, city, "yes/no," product type. You can count them, but averaging makes no sense (what's the "average" of red and blue?).

cards.title: Text, images, audio, video
cards.body: Tweets, photos, voice notes, clips. Massive and messy — this "unstructured" data is what powers modern AI: vision, speech, and ChatGPT.

cards.title: Even dates & locations count
cards.body: Timestamps, GPS coordinates, button clicks — all are data types too, each with its own uses (when? where? what did they tap?).

cards.title: Two everyday kinds
cards.body: The most common split you'll meet:

exercises.explain: Step count is a measurable number you can total and average. The others are categories.
Eye color is a label/group — a category. Height, weight, and age are measurable numbers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: photos, voice notes, and tweets are all data.
exercises.explain: True — images, audio, and text are all data types (the 'unstructured' kind that powers modern AI).

exercises.sentence: Age and price are examples of ___ data.
exercises.explain: Age and price are measurable numbers — quantitative data.

exercises.prompt: A shop records: customer age (28), city (Paris), and a photo of the receipt. How many different data TYPES is that?

options.text: Three: a number, a category, and an image

exercises.explain: Three distinct types: age = number, city = category, receipt photo = image. Real datasets mix types all the time.
